data.title: Cal-Maine expects Prepared Foods capacity to increase more than 30% over the next 18 to 24 months as pricing shifts toward structured arrangements
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) Q3 fiscal 2026

Management view

  • CEO Sherman Miller framed Cal-Maine’s strategy as “to compound intrinsic value per share over time through thoughtful portfolio evolution, efficient operations and prudent capital allocation,” while emphasizing mix shift, pricing structure changes, and scaling Prepared Foods to improve earnings durability in a cyclical egg market.
  • Miller highlighted portfolio mix changes: specialty eggs were “50.5% of total shell egg sales compared to 24.4%,” Prepared Foods were “9.5% of net sales compared to 0.8%,” and combined specialty plus Prepared Foods were “52.9% of net sales compared to 24%.”
  • Miller described market conditions as less disrupted than last year: “The average layer in flock is up about 2.2% year-over-year and depopulations are down 70.6% year-over-year,” adding that “retail volumes are up about 3% year-to-date,” while wholesale prices faced pressure as supply recovered and inventory building normalized.
  • Miller pointed to M&A as part of tighter integration across the value chain, citing “the acquisition of the shell egg, egg products and prepared foods assets of Creighton Brothers and Crystal Lake,” which he said adds “nearby liquid egg capacity” to support internal sourcing for Prepared Foods ingredients.
  • “For the third quarter of fiscal 2026, net sales were $667 million compared to $1.4 billion, down 53%,” said CFO Max Bowman, detailing that “diluted earnings per share were $1.06 compared to $10.38,” and noting Cal-Maine ended the quarter with “cash and temporary cash investments of $1.152 billion” and remained “virtually debt-free.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated the Prepared Foods buildout timeline: “We expect Prepared Foods capacity to increase more than 30% over the next 18 to 24 months,” CFO Bowman said, adding that margin recovery is expected to be “progressive… beginning in Q4” and “trending back towards baseline through fiscal 2027 and 2028 as scale and network efficiencies are realized.”
  • Bowman said Prepared Foods Q3 “represents a trough,” driven by “planned network optimization and expansion activities,” with “temporary downtime and under-absorption of fixed costs,” and he characterized demand as intact: “This is a function of execution timing, not structural weakness.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s messaging that temporary impacts “began late in the second quarter… and are expected to continue through the remainder of the fiscal year,” the current call added more explicit timing language around a Q4 recovery start and a multi-year path “through fiscal 2027 and 2028.”

Financial results

  • CALM reported “net sales… $667 million,” “gross profit… $119.3 million,” “operating income… $35.9 million,” and “net income attributable to Cal-Maine… $50.5 million,” CFO Bowman said.
  • Bowman attributed the profit compression primarily to price: gross profit declined “primarily driven by 56.5% lower shell egg selling prices,” partially offset by lower outside egg purchases as “our percentage produced to sold increased… to 91.5%.”
  • Segment detail included conventional egg sales of “$283.2 million” and specialty egg sales of “$289.1 million,” while Prepared Foods sales were “$63.6 million,” with Bowman adding that Crepini Foods sales rose “283%” within the Prepared Foods portfolio.
  • Capital return actions included a Q3 variable dividend of “approximately $0.36 per share,” and share repurchases of “329,830 shares… for a total of $24.3 million,” with “$350.8 million” remaining under the authorization.

Q&A

  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research LLC: Asked whether Q4 specialty pricing should be similar to Q3 given California price volatility; CEO Sherman Miller said specialty prices are “a lot more stable,” and CFO Max Bowman added “the vast majority… is either grain-based or fixed price type arrangement cost plus,” with roughly “about 12%” tied to the California cage-free market.
  • Heather Jones, Heather Jones Research LLC: Pressed on when Prepared Foods margins return to “baseline”; CFO Bowman said Q3 was a “trough quarter,” recovery should begin in “Q4 ’26,” but “that margin recovery will really start showing up towards the end of ’27 and… into ’28,” citing mix seasonality and continued shift toward “grain-based and longer-term pricing arrangements.”
  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens: Asked about conventional pricing and returns under hybrid pricing; CEO Miller said hybrid pricing reduces volatility with “trade-offs,” and CFO Bowman said they are “taking some off the top… and trading that for longer-term, more stable earnings,” while reiterating prior commentary that ROIC is “double digit, well above our cost of capital.”
  • Pooran Sharma, Stephens: Asked whether liquid eggs remain an M&A focus; Miller cited the Creighton Brothers deal’s “liquid egg capacity,” and Bowman referenced a capital allocation recap showing “about $1 billion of capital” deployed over the last 12 months, including “about 38% or $384 million” to dividends and “about $299 million” to acquisitions.
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about branded demand and contract opportunities; Miller reiterated category demand data and said cage-free projects “tee us up” for branded growth, while Bowman said specialty volume was “a record specialty quarter” and referenced “regional growth… into the fourth quarter and beyond.”
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Asked about feed cost risk; Miller said the company uses “grain warehousing basis locks or hedging strategies” and noted “grain-based agreements have offset the effect of grain price change.”
  • Benjamin Mayhew, BMO Capital Markets: Asked about specialty supply and promotions; Miller said the market is returning to promotion “routine” after shortage conditions, while emphasizing Cal-Maine is building supply via projects.
  • Benjamin Klieve, The Benchmark Company: Asked whether retailers are reconsidering contract pricing as commodity prices fell; Miller said retailer strategies were “tested” with prices ranging “up to $2.69 all the way down to $0.85 within the same quarter,” adding that pricing strategies “performed exactly like they were designed to.”
  • Benjamin Klieve, The Benchmark Company: Asked about Prepared Foods market size and fragmentation and whether acquisition pace continues; Miller responded, “we certainly have not topped out here,” and said Cal-Maine intends to stay “egg-centric… in the breakfast channel.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ sentiment was slightly negative to neutral, focusing on pricing durability (specialty and conventional), timing and magnitude of Prepared Foods margin recovery, promotional intensity in specialty, and customer behavior under low commodity prices.
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing stability and long-term positioning; Bowman described Prepared Foods as a “trough” and said “demand remains intact,” while Miller emphasized reduced volatility and that strategies “performed exactly like they were designed to.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management tone remained confident but provided more concrete timing detail on Prepared Foods recovery (Q4 start; benefits “towards the end of ’27… into ’28”), while analysts continued to probe the same core themes—hybrid pricing, Prepared Foods cadence, and specialty growth—now adding more emphasis on promotions and competitive supply in specialty.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Mix shift messaging strengthened: Q3 disclosed specialty at “50.5% of total shell egg sales” and specialty plus Prepared Foods at “52.9% of net sales,” compared with Q2 citing specialty at “44% of total shell egg sales” and specialty plus prepared foods at “46.4% of net sales.”
  • Prepared Foods narrative evolved from Q2’s expectation of temporary volume/cost impacts “through the remainder of the fiscal year” to Q3’s framing of Q3 as the “trough” and an expected “progressive recovery beginning in Q4,” with margin normalization pushed out “through fiscal 2027 and 2028.”
  • Analysts maintained consistent focus quarter-to-quarter on pricing structure (hybrid and cost-plus) and Prepared Foods profitability, while Q3 questions leaned more into specialty pricing mechanics (California linkage), promotions, and retailer reactions to sharply lower commodity prices.

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited ongoing volatility drivers including High Path AI, though with less disruption than last year; Miller said it is “still a reality,” but “not driving the same level of supply shock.”
  • Input-cost risk centered on grain, fertilizer, and fuel; Miller said consultants indicated “probably 90% of the inputs have already been locked” for fertilizer, and the company plans to mitigate with “grain warehousing basis locks or hedging strategies.”
  • Prepared Foods execution risk was explicitly acknowledged as transitional downtime and under-absorption; Bowman said near-term pressure is “largely volume-driven” and tied to the timing of network changes.

Final takeaway

Management portrayed Q3 as a period where lower shell egg pricing pressure was partially offset by a faster-evolving portfolio mix, more structured pricing arrangements, and continued investment in Prepared Foods capacity. Executives emphasized that Prepared Foods results reflected a planned “trough” tied to optimization work, with recovery expected to begin in Q4 and normalization “through fiscal 2027 and 2028,” while maintaining a virtually debt-free balance sheet, ongoing variable dividends, and share repurchases alongside selective egg-centric acquisitions such as Creighton Brothers and Crystal Lake.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chesapeake Utilities elevates Michael Galtman to CTO role to lead ERP
data.description:
• Chesapeake Utilities (CPK) announced that Michael Galtman, already part of the company, will take on a new role as CTO effective April 1 to lead ERP rollout while managing finance and development functions.
  • As part of this leadership change, Melissa Barnes was appointed as VP & CAO, responsible for accounting and financial reporting.

data.title: Gold and silver gain while dollar drops ahead of Trump's address on Iran
data.description:
Gold and silver futures rose for the fourth straight session Wednesday amid speculation over what President Trump will say in his prime-time speech tonight, with tentative hopes for a de-escalation of Middle East tensions that could lead to lower oil prices and alleviate concerns over central banks' rate increases.

The U.S. dollar fell for a second consecutive day, making greenback-priced bullion more attractive for holders ​of other currencies.

"An end to the ‌conflict ⁠could prove a double-edged sword" for gold, IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note. "On one hand, a lasting peace agreement would remove the geopolitical safe-haven bid that supported prices in the run-up to the conflict," while on the other hand, lower oil prices and easing inflation "could ​revive expectations of 2026 ​Fed cuts," which could ⁠help gold.

Precious metals prices held their ground as U.S. retail ​sales rose solidly in February and the ADP's national employment report ⁠showed U.S. private payrolls increased steadily in March.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery jumped 2.9% to $4,783.20/oz, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) rose 1.6% to $75.867/oz, the highest settlement values for both metals in two weeks.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

Outlook

data.title: Sleep Number seeks rescue financing to avoid bankruptcy -- report
data.description:
Sleep Number (SNBR) is seeking rescue financing to prevent bankruptcy as the company struggles with sluggish sales due to a weak housing market, competitive pressures, tariffs, and declining traffic to its showrooms.

In its most recent quarterly results, the mattress manufacturer reported an 8% decline in fourth-quarter sales and a $32M drop in gross profit due to a $9.6M inventory obsolescence charge.

Crucially, in its annual report filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission last month, the company warned that it had “substantial doubt” it could continue as a going concern due to “high debt, weak sales, and expected financial covenant breaches.”

During the company’s earnings call with analysts, CFO Amy O’Keefe said that the company will work with advisors to address the company’s credit facility and “other opportunities,” including refinancing its credit facility and explore “inbound interest.” Sleep Number (SNBR) has hired Guggenheim Securities to explore options.

Over just the past month, shares have lost more than 71% of their value, recently enduring an 11-day losing streak that brought the stock to an all-time low of $1.60 per share.    

Shares are down another 8% in Thursday's after hours trading.

data.title: Stitch Fix resumes share repurchase program
data.description:
• Stitch Fix (SFIX) on Wednesday said that it has resumed share repurchases under its existing program, initially authorized in January 2022.
  • The company said it repurchased about 4.5 million Class A shares for $15 million between March 17 and April 1, leaving approximately $105 million available under the $150 million program.
  • Repurchases may be conducted through the open market, privately negotiated transactions, or Rule 10b5-1 plans, at the company’s discretion.
  • SFIX +2.94% after hours to $3.49.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: South Pacific Metals appoints Simon Jackson to Board and Rebecca Moriarty as CFO
data.description:
• South Pacific Metals (SPMC:CA) announced on Wednesday that it had appointed Simon Jackson to its Board of Directors effective March 30, 2026, and that Rebecca Moriarty had been named CFO effective April 30, 2026.
  • Jackson had been an experienced mining executive with senior roles across the full project lifecycle and board positions at companies listed on the TSX, TSX Venture Exchange, and ASX.
  • Jackson had been serving as Chairman of Predictive Discovery Limited as well as a director of several other publicly listed companies.
  • Moriarty brings over 20 years of mining industry experience, combining technical and business accounting expertise. She had formerly worked as a manager at PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, focusing on resource companies.
  • Moriarty will be succeeding Scott Kelly as CFO, with Kelly having planned to resign effective April 30, 2026.
  • The company also had granted 1,860,000 restricted share units, subject to TSX Venture Exchange approval.
  • Of the RSUs, 992,500 had been set to vest on March 31, 2027, and 867,500 on March 31, 2028, with each unit entitling the holder to shares or a cash equivalent under the plan.
  • Shares +6.59%.

data.title: Coinbase CLO sees Clarity Act in 48 hours: Will BTC break or shake?
data.description:
Coinbase (COIN) Chief Legal Officer (CLO) Paul Grewal said in a FOX Business interview that a deal on the CLARITY Act, especially around stablecoin yields, could be finalized within the next 48 hours, possibly by Friday. 

In the interview, Grewal said that the market structure bill is important, as it will define which tokens will fall under the SEC or CFTC. This comes after U.S. President Donald Trump had asked Congress to pass the TRANSPARENCY Act quickly, showing a strong push for clear crypto rules. 

However, reports from Crypto In America and journalist Eleanor Terrett, which cited a Senate spokesperson, said the bill text will not be released this week, as lawmakers want to avoid giving opponents a chance to slow the process before the upcoming markup.

Discussions are still ongoing between crypto firms and banking groups, especially after concerns over an earlier draft involving Senator Thom Tillis, Senator Angela Alsobrooks, and the White House, reported Crypto in America.

In a positive development, Moody’s gave a Ba2 rating to New Hampshire’s $100M Bitcoin-backed bond. These developments are important for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) because clearer rules and more institutional use can build long-term trust among investors.

As per the TradingView chart, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is trading around $68.13K, down about 4% this week. The price is in a short downtrend, with only a small recovery so far.

RSI is near 45, showing weak momentum, and MACD is still negative, which means selling pressure is still there. But the indicators are starting to flatten, so the selling pressure may be slowing.

The key support is around $65K, and resistance is near $70K. If BTC breaks above $70K, it can move toward $76K. If it falls below the support level, it may drop toward $63K again.

Altcoins like Ethereum (ETH-USD), XRP (XRP-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), and BNB (BNB-USD) are following BTC’s price pattern. They may stay weak or range-bound for now, with weekly crashes between 2% and 8%.

If the CLARITY Act and related bills move forward, it can make crypto rules clearer and improve the market sentiment from extreme fear (8 levels) to the neutral zone.
